comment.body:
theta risk, volatility, and strategy of course.
there was a trade when i chose friday expos, only bcs I was prepared that i will maybe hold them over the week, but due to the high price swings ,i don't see too much edge, bad sortino.
if i expect vol to go down (like yesterday, as MMs are longer downside vol), and we are in a MM short fly pattern, I mind theta risk, and I estimated that maybe I will need to hold those contracts very long, so the theta will kill the premium, so i went for a safer bet. 
With 0DTEs one has to be very accurate with timing. Yesterday, I felt that I can't time the reversal perfectly, so I chosed 1DTE.

I like OTMs for longer-term trades, cheaper and you get better convexity. But they only have extrinsic value, so the premium decay is faster.
For intraday trading I like ATMs and ITMs more, bcs ATMs have the highest gamma so it gains profit the fastest, and ITMs have intrinsic value that doesn't affected by vol and time. 
I use ATM when conviction is high, and use ITM when I know that I'm a bit off with timing, and use OTM when I go for longer-term trades...oooor when I'm 101% sure on a 0DTE intraday trade, but it is risky.
Also 0DTE OTMs are good for vol trading and hedging purposes for stock or futs positions

comment.body:
INTRADAY REALIZED VOL SCRIPT:

//@version=5
indicator("GKYZ Volatility", shorttitle="GKYZ Vol. Est.", overlay=false)


lookback = input.int(5, title="Lookback Period", minval=1)  


f_calcDailyRV() =>


    window          = lookback
    trading_periods = 390


    log_hoD = math.log(high / open)
    log_loD = math.log(low  / open)
    log_coD = math.log(close / open)


    log_ocD    = math.log(open / nz(close[1]))
    log_oc_sqD = log_ocD * log_ocD


    log_ccD    = math.log(close / nz(close[1]))
    log_cc_sqD = log_ccD * log_ccD


    rsD = log_hoD*(log_hoD - log_coD) + log_loD*(log_loD - log_coD)


    close_volD = math.sum(log_cc_sqD, window) / (window - 1)
    open_volD  = math.sum(log_oc_sqD, window) / (window - 1)
    window_rsD = math.sum(rsD, window)        / (window - 1)
    kD         = 0.34 / (1.34 + (window + 1.0)/(window - 1.0))
    rvD        = math.sqrt((open_volD + kD*close_volD + (1 - kD)*window_rsD) * trading_periods)


dailyRV = request.security(syminfo.tickerid, "1", f_calcDailyRV())


plot(dailyRV, title="GKYZ Volatility", color=#970ef3, linewidth=2)
comment.raw_body:
INTRADAY REALIZED VOL SCRIPT:

//@version=5
indicator("GKYZ Volatility", shorttitle="GKYZ Vol. Est.", overlay=false)


lookback = input.int(5, title="Lookback Period", minval=1)  


f_calcDailyRV() =>


    window          = lookback
    trading_periods = 390


    log_hoD = math.log(high / open)
    log_loD = math.log(low  / open)
    log_coD = math.log(close / open)


    log_ocD    = math.log(open / nz(close[1]))
    log_oc_sqD = log_ocD * log_ocD


    log_ccD    = math.log(close / nz(close[1]))
    log_cc_sqD = log_ccD * log_ccD


    rsD = log_hoD*(log_hoD - log_coD) + log_loD*(log_loD - log_coD)


    close_volD = math.sum(log_cc_sqD, window) / (window - 1)
    open_volD  = math.sum(log_oc_sqD, window) / (window - 1)
    window_rsD = math.sum(rsD, window)        / (window - 1)
    kD         = 0.34 / (1.34 + (window + 1.0)/(window - 1.0))
    rvD        = math.sqrt((open_volD + kD*close_volD + (1 - kD)*window_rsD) * trading_periods)


dailyRV = request.security(syminfo.tickerid, "1", f_calcDailyRV())


plot(dailyRV, title="GKYZ Volatility", color=#970ef3, linewidth=2)

comment.body:
so it estimates that your returns come from one of two hidden regimes.
defined by their own Gaussian distribution. I guess you have read my post about them. 
The means and variances tell you what the typical return and volatility are in each regime. For example, if state 1 has a mean of –0.00025, this means that when the model is in state 1, the typical avg return is –0.025% per observation. 
Then we have the variance of returns within that state. A very low variance means the returns in that state are very consistent, while a high variance would mean they fluctuate a lot.
The state sequence is all in state 1 means that the model finds no evidence of switching regimes during the analysed (1week, 1hourly candles) period.
And also we have a transition probability matrix, that tells me the probability of staying in the same state or switching to the other state at the next time step.

comment.body:
this is a very complex topic. It is volatility modelign btw. GJR-GARCH, Heston-Nandi, Rough Bergomin (still under research), etc. by that you can forecast possible path for the volatility, or even (if you have a good estimation rate) use it for arbitrage trading between rough vol model prices and traditional models prices.. some funds do that.
So you get an expected volatility paths with a distirbution like as i attached. and you can do this via deep learning as well... i prefer that.
and this gives an idea, then you simply just look at the vol complex, VX futs, options, also you can model the vomma surface, etc. 
you derive the vol impact from the positioning, you can simulate possible paths, then get a distirbution again. this time, for the price. 

and the instinct, when you are thinking about the very recent flows, how one came from the other, and what the market wants. bcs the best deep learning tool is your brain at the end,, all these tools only help you to see patterns. 
and you make a decision to a bias. will post about these methods more, but the thinking process can be observed in my posts. i try to show that, bcs thats how you can learn

comment.body:
i had a put from feb19, i kept rolling a few portion of it, while taking profit out of it at the same time.
my entries (when i do manually, like what you saw in the p ast two weeks), are tied pretty much for the reversion lvls and the lvls i give you + intraday vol
okay, i have a machine learning based momentum metric, but i did a very simplified yet accurate enough indicator for you too, in order to get a better edge.

so the reversion lvls are basically the different slices of the probability distribution. it gives you literally a probability + risk lvl, that confirms or disproves the recent momentum.
then you have the positioning + orderflow. 
this gives you an idea about the dynamics that can be waited. 
longer gamma = slows down the momentum, absorbs intraday vol,
vanna flip lines, etc. 
and you look for anomalies at the lvls, and in the order flow to catch if the price doesn't behave there as it should, mechanically.

order flow can lie, bcs big players, funds like to scale into positions in small sizes in order to avoid catching others attention (like they did on tuesday) - i knew that they bought, bcs i have friends from here and there, and i just heard. nothing special.

if i wanna go for a reversion trade on the call side, i want convexity on the calls, and i want room for the momentum. i patiently wait for a certain lvl where the reversion probability is higher, and we get gamma on the calls to gain profit. like it was on thursday, i didn't rush into any trades.

comment.body:
know the instrument you trade with: option, futures, stocks, how they behave... know the underlying's dynamics how it interacts with others.
don't trade too much assets, learn a sector, or just one or too assets and master that. edge can be found anywhere.
You have to decide what you are: an investor, or a speculant.
Do you build a portfolio (what model), and what is your strategy. also you have to know how to hedge your portfolio.
then learn at least basic macro, how the economy works (Ray Dalio has a very basic but very nice presentation on that "how the economic machine works in 30 mins" check youtube)
and most importantly, you have to day up to date with current market theories in order to read the tape in the present times.

This is why I wrote educational posts on topics like what i dropped.. not greeks that is very fancy and popular nowadays (over-emphatized)

comment.body:
very different, bcs my algo determines the size of it, and its greeks... so it adds or/and decreases the portfolio exposures. 
like, I sold a ratio put spread 1:3 with March expo, it is basically a spot down/vol down bet, and as the put goes ITM, from vega and theta terms it is basically a risk reversal trade with long vanna, short vega, long theta, but the delta is negative. if i add long OTM calls, my vol and theta exposures can be mitigated, or even canceled out, but if the time to maturity is shorter, it won't affect the net vega and theta exposures... or if i chose an even longer dated maturity, it will have more vega impact ...+ their derivatives of course. so it is complex,

but for shorter term trades, one simply determines what is the bet: what is the timeframe you are thinking about and what is the vol that you bet on. long vol, short vol, beside long delta short delta bets.
and you manage the theta risk with lets say, longer maturity, but now you get more vega impact potentially... so it depends on the strategy.

comment.body:
if you mean the reversion lvls, those are vol based.
the other lvls (like zero vanna, or other border lines wher stabilization or destabilization can be expected etc.) are posiitoning and projected vol+dealer flow impact based.
momentum mased lvls are combinations of probability and positioning

comment.body:
Skew is the IV across the strikes on a certain day. Nothing more, nothing less.
In the middle you have the ATM strike, on the left-side there are the OTM puts/ITM calls, and on the right side there are the OTM calls/ITM puts 
Here I covered the basic of it, but you will certainly get an educational post on that as well 
https://x.com/alma18499/status/1798650957875523611

comment.body:
What is IV? (in short)
It is the volatility risk premium on the contract. Indirectly reflecting the supply/demand of that contract.
Bcs the higher the demand for a certain contract, the more people are betting on that contract going ITM into its expo.
And if the demand grows, the price increases. Why? bcs the seller will have loss on that contract if the buyer wins. So he increases the price.
And bcs volatility is the SD of the logreturns, the higher the vol, the higher range of returns are expected on that contract. 
So the higher risk the sellers has that the contract will expire ITM. So he will ask for higher premia.

comment.body:
What we traders are interested in in terms of skew, is its relative changes.
You chose the tenor, or date you are interested in, and check how the skew looked like yesterday, 1 weekago, after the last OpEx, vs today etc.
And you can check where the sentiment goes. 
We usually meassure the slopeness of the skew by simply taking the 22 delta put vs ATM segment, or 5 delta put vs 22 delta put, and the 22 delta call vs ATM, and the ATM itself...
of course, some plp use the 25 deltas, others the 20 deltas instead of 22 delta... again instead of 5 deltas one might use the 10 deltas... 
(I prefer the 22 delta, bcs thats where vanna peaks in avg, and 5 delta, bcs that is used many times for vega strats when hedging tails)

And so by comparing how the slopeness of these segments are changed over the time, you will get an idea about the supply/demand of the customer flow.

If you chose multi expiry chart, thats basically the 3D chart

and this is what really shows you the market sentiment in terms of volatility, not the VIX.
bcs it tells you how the supply and demand changes for insurances for certain delta contracts. 
We are looking for sudden high convexities on the tails, or declines that are simply contradictionary from the present sentiment perspective we experience

comment.body:
also 22 delta put vs call skew is a close enough proxy for the vanna and charm exposures.
bcs customer longer skew = MM longer vanna, shorter charm
customer shorter skew = MM shorter vanna, longer charm
